straight_beat.name: Kenji Hollingsworth
straight_beat.column:
The Mariners’ 6-2 loss to the Astros yesterday was a game that defied narrative. By the numbers, Seattle’s offense looked sharp, out-hitting their opponents in key categories. They finished with a .268 team batting average against Houston’s .241, a difference that should’ve translated to runs. But here’s the rub: the Astros’ bullpen allowed just two earned runs over the final three innings, and the Mariners’ offense, for all its efficiency, couldn’t capitalize on the opportunities.  

Take the lineup. Luis Torrens led off the first with a double, then stole second. His .318 average is the highest on the team, yet he’s walked 37 times this season—more than any Mariner since 2015. That’s a sign of

optimist.name: Rainy Beckerman
optimist.column:
The Astros took yesterday’s game, but the Mariners didn’t just lose a ballgame—they lost a moment. A moment where the team’s heart, still raw from the 2022 breakthrough, beat louder than the scoreboard. Sure, the Astros won 6-2, but let’s not mistake that for a verdict. This is a team that’s still learning how to win consistently, and every loss is a lesson in disguise.  

Look at the way J-Rod stepped into the batter’s box yesterday. He’s not the same player who hit 30 homers last season, but there he was, swinging with the same fire, chasing pitches outside the strike zone like he’s got a personal vendetta against the umpires. He struck out four times, sure, but those swings? They’re the kind of swings that build muscle memory. You can’t teach that kind of grit. It’s the kind of grit that’ll translate into walks, singles, and eventually, runs.  

And let’s talk about the bullpen. They’re still figuring out how to close games, but the fact that they’re in the thick of the AL West race means they’re not just surviving—they’re competing. The front office hasn’t given up on this roster, and that’s

pessimist.name: Walt Varga
pessimist.column:
Another quiet offense, another wasted start. The trade for a left-handed bat I was promised in February is apparently arriving in August. Of 2031.  

The Mariners’ loss to the Astros yesterday wasn’t just another defeat—it was a textbook example of why this team feels like a weather report in March. The offense, as reliable as a Seattle rainstorm in July, managed to muster just two runs. Not two runs, mind you, but two runs that came from a solo home run by a player who’s been on this roster since the last century. The rest of the lineup? A collection of ghosts who’ve never hit a ball out of the infield since they’ve been here. It’s not just the bats—it
